Title: Businesses expect softer outlook in Q2 2026: Statistics Department
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Businesses anticipate a softer second quarter 2026 (Q2 2026) outlook with the confidence indicator easing to negative 1.8 per cent from 4 per cent in the previous quarter, according to the Statistics Department.
In a statement today, chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the construction, wholesale and retail trade, and industry sectors are expecting less favourable business performance in the quarter reviewed, indicating that businesses remain cautious amid a challenging business environment and global uncertainties.
"The services sector continues to maintain a positive outlook for Q2 2026, benefiting from continued government support measures that preserve consumers' purchasing power and facilitate business activities, particularly in domestic-driven service sectors," he said in conjunction with the department's release of the Business Tendency Statistics for Q2 2026.
Mohd Uzir said the construction sector turned pessimistic, with the confidence indicator registering negative 9.5 per cent in Q2 2026 against 1.4 per cent in the previous quarter.
The confidence indicator for the wholesale and retail trade sector declined to negative 7.5 per cent in the quarter reviwed while the industry sector projected a weaker performance, with a confidence indicator of negative 3.7 per cent against a positive 3.3 per cent in Q1 2026.
"The services sector expects business performance to remain optimistic, with a confidence indicator of 6.2 per cent against 6.6 per cent in the previous quarter," he said.
Mohd Uzir said mixed business expectations were also observed across states, indicating varying economic prospects.
Johor recorded a confidence indicator of 1.2 per cent, Kedah (3.2 per cent), Kelantan (3.8 per cent), Negeri Sembilan (1.9 per cent), Perak (0.9 per cent), Selangor (0.2 per cent), and Terengganu (5.5 per cent).
A pessimitic lull is over Melaka with the confidence indicator at negative 9.1 per cent, Pahang (- 7.1 per cent), Penang (- 5.2 per cent), Perlis (- 7.1 per cent), Sabah (- 7.6 per cent), Sarawak (- 5.9 per cent), Kuala Lumpur (- 2.8 per cent), Labuan (- 9.5 per cent), and Putrajaya (- 2.3 per cent).
Business sentiment between April and September 2026 remains optimistic with a net balance metric easing to 7.9 per cent from 10.8 per cent previously while the industry sector has a net balance of 13 per cent, up from 6.2 per cent previously.
Services sector business sentiment remains positive, with a 7.5 per cent net balance against 20.6 per cent from January to June 2026.
Mohd Uzir expects a decline in business conditions in the construction sector, as reflected by a negative 5.4 per cent net balance versus 12 per cent previously.
Concurrently, subdued sentiment was recorded in the wholesale and retail trade sector, with a negative 1 per cent net balance against 6.9 per cent previously.
About 33 per cent of businesses expect Q2 2026 gross revenue to rise, 43 per cent unchanged and 24 per cent expects a decline, resulting in a net balance of 9 per cent for the reference quarter.
Meanwhile, 14.7 per cent of businesses project employment to rise, 75.8 per cent expect to maintain their workforce and 9.5 per cent expect a fall, contributing a 5.3 per cent net balance in Q2 2026, he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Storekeeper acquitted of trafficking over 3kg of drugs in Tawau
Article:
TAWAU: A vehicle storage facility storekeeper was today discharged and acquitted by the magistrate's court here of trafficking more than 3kg of syabu after the prosecution withdrew the charge against him.
Magistrate Don Stiwin Malanjum made the ruling after the prosecution decided not to proceed with the charge under Section 39B(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 faced by Hadriansyah Sabran, 46.
Hadriansyah had been accused of trafficking 3,148g of methamphetamine at a vehicle storage warehouse along Jalan Sin Onn here at 7.30pm on March 13.
The offence carries the death penalty or life imprisonment and whipping, upon conviction.
Earlier, defence lawyer Datuk Ram Singh told the court that representations had been submitted to the state prosecution director on April 27 and 28, together with closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage and supporting documents, after serious concerns were raised over the integrity of the arrest and investigation.
He said the defence maintained that the accused had been framed from the outset, alleging that the drugs were planted by the raiding team before Hadriansyah was unlawfully arrested and charged.
The defence also claimed that the accused and Smart Bid Auction Sdn Bhd had been publicly linked to drug trafficking activities through official media statements related to the case, affecting their reputation and raising concerns among financial institutions and business partners.
Ram said his client had suffered hardship, humiliation, emotional distress, loss of liberty and reputational damage following what was alleged to be an unlawful arrest and prosecution.
He said that after reviewing the representations and CCTV footage submitted by the defence, the state prosecution director decided to withdraw the trafficking charge.
The court recorded the withdrawal before ordering Hadriansyah to be discharged and acquitted.
However, Hadriansyah, who also faced a separate charge under Section 5(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, was sentenced to nine months' imprisonment from the date of his arrest on March 13 after pleading guilty to the offence.
The prosecution was conducted by prosecuting officer Assistant Superintendent Nur Intan Jamrin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UOA Development earnings drops 43pct to RM41.9mil in Q1 on lower revenue
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: UOA Development Bhd's net profit fell 43.3 per cent to RM41.94 million in the first quarter ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 FY26) from RM73.95 million a year earlier, dragged by lower revenue and weaker fair value gains.
Revenue declined 25.1 per cent to RM113.86 million, from RM152.1 million previously.
The group's revenue is primarily derived from the progressive recognition of ongoing development projects, namely Bamboo Hills Residences, Aster Hill, and Duo Tower.
Earnings per share fell to 1.58 sen compared with 2.82 sen in Q1 FY25, the group said in a filing to Bursa Malaysia.
On prospects, UOA said new property sales for the period ended March 31, 2026, totalled RM130.7 million, with Aethera Residences, Duo Tower, Bamboo Hills Residences, and Aster Hill as the main contributors.
Unbilled sales stood at RM612.4 million at quarter-end, which will be recognised progressively in the coming years.
The group said it continues to explore strategic opportunities for future growth and expansion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eight men, including six tourists, claim trial to possessing drugs in hotel room
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Eight men, including six foreigners, pleaded not guilty at the magistrate's court today to two counts of possessing ketamine and methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) last Sunday.
Malaysians Ivan Wong Yi Xian, 24, and Chong King Jun, 36, claimed trial after the charges were read to them before magistrate Nurul Izzah Hasan Basri.
The six foreign defendants – Jensen Soh Kah Yi, 34; Lam Hoi Leung, 30; Hendry Gunawan, 28; Tang Waei, 41; Tran Quang Ha, 26; and Yeo Wei Seng, 37, also pleaded not guilty.
On the first count, the group is accused of common intention in possessing 1.5g of ketamine inside a room at a hotel in Kuala Lumpur at 4am on May 24.
The charge is framed under Section 12(2) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, punishable under Section 12(3) of the same Act, and read together with Section 34 of the Penal Code.
The offence carries a maximum fine of RM100,000, up to five years in jail, or both, upon conviction.
On the second count, they are accused of possessing 0.7g of MDMA at the same time and location.
Deputy public prosecutor Nurul Farhanah Arul Hisham appeared for the prosecution.
National Legal Aid Foundation (YBGK) counsel Roshvinder Singh represented the two Malaysians, while lawyer Yong Jei Bing represented the six foreign nationals.
Roshvinder pleaded for a low bail amount for the Malaysians, noting that they work as a salesperson and a beauty salon employee, respectively.
Yong requested bail for the foreigners, stating they were in Malaysia as tourists and did not come from wealthy backgrounds.
Nurul Izzah granted bail at RM6,000 with one surety each for the two Malaysian defendants but denied bail for the six foreigners.
The court fixed August 27 for mention and document submission.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No roadblocks during Aidiladha - Loke
Article:
KAJANG: No roadblocks will be conducted during the Hari Raya Aidiladha period to avoid worsening traffic congestion, Transport Minister Datuk Seri Anthony Loke said today.
Instead, the Road Transport Department (RTD) will deploy 2,000 enforcement personnel nationwide this week as part of efforts to improve road safety and reduce accidents during the festive and school holiday season.
Loke said the operation would adopt the "Perception of Being Caught" (POBC) approach, where enforcement officers are stationed at strategic locations to encourage motorists to drive more cautiously.
"Our objective is not to issue summonses, but to ensure road users become more cautious when they see enforcement officers on the roads," he told a press conference after visiting the Carro Technology Sdn Bhd's MV15 vehicle inspection centre here today.
He said the festive operation would involve integrated enforcement with police, focusing on accident-prone hotspots and inspections of commercial vehicles, particularly express buses.
According to Loke, RTD personnel would conduct monitoring and inspections at bus depots and terminals before buses depart to ensure drivers and vehicles comply with safety requirements.
He said enforcement officers would inspect drivers' licences, road tax, insurance coverage and Public Service Vehicle (PSV) licences, while random drug screening tests would also be conducted on bus drivers.
"We want to ensure the drivers are fit and in good condition, and that the buses are roadworthy," he said.
Loke stressed that bus operators also have a responsibility to monitor their drivers and vehicles, warning that companies could face action from the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD) if they fail to comply with safety requirements.
He added that operators could face licence suspension if their drivers are found committing offences or violating regulations.
Asked about a viral video allegedly showing an express bus driver pouring liquid into a bottle while driving, Loke said RTD's inspections on bus drivers were carried out consistently, including periodic drug screening tests.
"Our officers cannot inspect every driver all the time, which is why bus companies also have responsibilities under their operating licence conditions," he said.
He said the same integrated enforcement strategy implemented during previous festive periods would continue this Aidiladha to help reduce accidents amid the expected increase in traffic volume.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang, Johor, Selangor, Sarawak, KL account for 88.9pct of April exports - DOSM
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Penang, Johor, Selangor, Sarawak and Kuala Lumpur dominated Malaysia's exports, accounting for 88.9 per cent of April 2026's total, according to the Statistics Department Malaysia (DOSM).
In the Export-Import Statistics by State April 2026 report released by DOSM today, chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said exports in April 2026 reached RM182.7 billion against RM154 billion a year ago, a RM49.2 billion year-on-year (y-o-y) increase.
Penang was the top exporter, contributing 45.1 per cent, followed by Johor (19.7 per cent), Selangor (14.6 per cent), Sarawak (5.3 per cent) and WP Kuala Lumpur (4.2 per cent).
He said the increase was attributed to the higher exports in Penang (+RM33.5 billion), Johor (+RM9.7 billion), WP Kuala Lumpur (+RM3.5 billion), Selangor (+RM2.5 billion) and Sarawak (+RM490.1 million).
Other state contributors include Perak (+RM472.2 million), Terengganu (+RM404.6 million), Sabah (+RM188.3 million), Perlis (+RM26.7 million) and Negri Sembilan (+RM25.2 million).
However, exports decreased in Pahang by RM573.5 million, Labuan (-RM289.1 million), Kedah (-RM188.6 million), Melaka (-RM158.2 million) and Kelantan (-RM11.8 million).
Meanwhile, Mohd Uzir said April 2026's imports rose by 20 per cent to RM25.6 billion.
The increase was attributed by the higher imports in most states such as Johor (+RM18.6 billion), Penang (+RM9.6 billion), Sarawak (+RM517.6 million), Perak (+RM322.5 million), Kedah (+RM294.8 million), and Labuan (+RM289.1 million).
Oher states that contribute to the import increase were Sabah (+RM211.6 million), Terengganu (+RM165.4 million), Negri Sembilan (+RM127.0 million), Pahang (+RM58.2 million) and Perlis (+RM40.9 million).
Imports fell in Selangor by RM2 billion, WP Kuala Lumpur (-RM1.3 billion), Kelantan (-RM27.1 million) and Melaka (-RM8.0 million).
Johor dominated Malaysia's imports with a share of 28 per cent, followed by Penang (25.3 per cent), Selangor (23.4 per cent), Kuala Lumpur (7.8 per cent) and Kedah (4.4 per cent).
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hong Leong Capital net profit drops 7.7pct to RM10mil in Q3
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Capital Bhd net profit slipped 7.7 per cent to RM10 million in the third quarter ended March 31, 2026 from 10.9 million a year ago.
Revenue fell three per cent to RM76.8 million from RM79 million.
For the nine-month period, the company's net profit rose 14.6 per cent to RM46.76 million from RM40.8 million, while revenue climbed one per cent to RM256.9 million from RM254 million.
Hong Leong Capital delivered an improved net profit growth of 15 per cent to RM46.8 million.
This improved performance was largely driven by stronger contributions from treasury and markets, alongside higher returns from equity investments.
The company's book value per share increased by one per cent to RM4.39 from RM4.33 last year.
Hong Leong Capital's chairman Tan Kong Khoon said the global economic outlook remains volatile as the ongoing Middle East conflict disrupts energy supply chains and elevates uncertainty to unprecedented levels.
He said the prolonged crisis carries the risk of sustained energy price shocks and exacerbated inflationary pressures that could contract global trade.
"Despite these external headwinds, the Malaysian capital market is expected to remain resilient, anchored by robust domestic institutional liquidity, steady corporate earnings and the ongoing execution of national structural reforms.
"Hong Leong Capital will continue to execute our strategic priorities by scaling our digital capabilities and deepening our global partnerships," he said.
Tan added that the company remains committed to launching new products through partnerships and accelerating artificial intelligence-driven transformations to drive both operational excellence and revenue growth.
By focusing on tailored solutions for affluent and small and medium enterprise segments, he said the company aims to broaden our revenue base while ensuring a seamless experience for all clients.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SMJ Energy rejects claims Sabah shortchanged in SAMUR deal
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Sabah continues to receive consistent financial returns from its strategic equity participation in the Sabah Ammonia Urea (SAMUR) project.
Through SMJ Energy Sdn Bhd, the Sabah government owns a 25 per cent equity interest in SAMUR, the company that owns and operates the SAMUR complex through a commercial collaboration agreement (CCA) with Petronas.
Since the signing of the share purchase agreement on April 25, 2023, dividends have been flowing into Sabah's coffers through SMJ Energy Sdn Bhd.
"To suggest that Sabah receives 'less than what is due' from SAMUR is baseless as it disregards the state government's drive, favourable commercial terms and strategic decisions that successfully secured Sabah's ownership in one of the region's most important petrochemical assets," SMJ Energy said in a statement.
The investment vehicle said SAMUR is recognised as the largest single-train granular fertiliser facility in Southeast Asia and the third-largest fertiliser plant in the Asia-Pacific region.
Built on Sabah soil and powered by Sabah gas, the project has positioned the state as an important player in the regional fertiliser and petrochemical industry.
"The positive impact of SAMUR extends well beyond the plant itself. Sipitang Oil and Gas Industrial Park (SOGIP), with SAMUR as an anchor project, has attracted substantial committed investments and is generating significant employment opportunities for Sabahans across engineering, logistics, infrastructure, housing, hospitality and technical services.
"This will continue to grow over time."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: France reports seven heatwave-linked deaths
Article:
PARIS: The French government said Tuesday that seven people were reported to have died in connection to the recent heatwave baking much of western Europe, five of which were drownings.
"What I can say today is that there have been seven deaths directly or indirectly related to the heat," government spokesperson Maud Bregeon told television broadcaster TF1.
Bregeon added that the figures and specific causes of death would need "to be clarified once the episode we are currently experiencing has come to an end."
The heatwave that has already seen record-breaking temperatures across the country was expected to intensify Tuesday, with eight departments in western France placed on orange heat alert – a first for the month of May.
Bregeon's office told AFP that the drowning incidents occurred in different areas across France, from southeast Lyon to the Atlantic coast.
High temperatures on Monday drove many people to the country's beaches to cool off in the water, even though lifeguard supervision is not due to start in many areas until July.
"We were just wondering this morning whether the beach was supervised," Thomas Dupuy told AFP while visiting a beach in the southwestern city of Anglet with his two young children.
"I'm extremely careful for myself, for my children who can't swim yet," he added
"We know the currents can pull you out, the Atlantic beaches are dangerous."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former convict, groom-to-be claim trial to raping and molesting teenager
Article:
MUAR: A former convict and an expectant groom pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court today to separate charges of raping and sexually assaulting a 17-year-old girl two months ago.
Muhammad Haziq Aiman Bahrin, 26, and Muhammad Haikal Abdullah, 23, claimed trial after the charges were read out separately before judge Mohd Khairi Haron.
Haziq, who was released early this year after serving a two-year prison sentence and whipping for rape, faced a new charge of physical sexual assault against the teenager.
He allegedly committed the offence at his home in the district between 3am and 4am in March.
The charge was framed under Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017, which carries a maximum 20-year jail sentence and whipping upon conviction.
Meanwhile, Haikal, a lorry driver, was charged under Section 376(1) of the Penal Code for raping the same victim at his home here between midnight and 3am in March.
The offence carries a maximum prison term of 20 years and whipping.
According to the facts of the case, Haikal had met the victim just an hour before inviting her out for a meal, but instead took her to his home, where he allegedly raped her.
Following that incident, the victim contacted a female friend who brought her to her home. However, the victim was then allegedly molested by Haziq.
The incidents came to light when the victim's mother noticed her daughter appearing depressed, prompting the teenager to reveal her ordeal.
Deputy public prosecutor Diyana Najihah Muhamed Fauzi prosecuted while both accused were unrepresented.
The court granted bail at RM8,000 each with conditions that they report to the nearest police station and refrain from contacting the victim or prosecution witnesses.
Khairi fixed July 15 for mention, document submission, and the appointment of legal counsel.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Balik kampung traffic builds up at Kelantan border ahead of Aidiladha
Article:
GUA MUSANG: Traffic heading into Kelantan via Gua Musang has begun building up today as more balik kampung travellers returned home ahead of Hari Raya Aidiladha tomorrow, with congestion stretching about 4km at the state border.
District police chief Superintendent Sik Choon Foo said vehicle volume had increased compared with yesterday, although traffic remained under control.
The congestion was concentrated along the Gua Musang-Kuala Lipis route near Kampung Mentara at the Pahang-Kelantan border, where vehicles slowed before entering Gua Musang town.
"Traffic conditions remain manageable, particularly around Kampung Mentara, despite the increase in vehicle volume. We are also monitoring slower movement at the Bendahara Junction traffic lights heading towards Kuala Krai," he said when contacted today.
Sik said traffic personnel had been deployed at key locations to manage congestion and carry out dispersal measures when necessary.
Motorists are advised to follow traffic regulations, maintain a safe distance and plan their journeys carefully during the festive travel period.
Checks 2pm found traffic moving steadily but slowly between Kampung Mentara and Kampung Lepan Jaya as vehicles streamed into Kelantan for Aidiladha.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's ties with Iran helped secure fuel supply via Strait of Hormuz - Anwar
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said Malaysia's strong diplomatic ties with Iran helped ensure the passage of the country's oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz amid escalating tensions in the Middle East.
Anwar said he is expected to receive a telephone call from the President of Iran at 6pm today, describing the communication as part of Malaysia's ongoing engagement with the country during a period of geopolitical uncertainty.
He said Malaysia's relationship with Iran was rooted not only in diplomatic cooperation, but also in humanitarian concerns and a shared commitment to justice following attacks linked to the Zionist Israeli regime and its allies.
"We express our sympathy because they were attacked and several of their cities were destroyed, similar to what happened in Gaza and now Lebanon," he said in his speech here today.
Earlier, Anwar presented 260 cows for korban to communities across the Permatang Pauh parliamentary constituency in conjunction with Aidiladha celebrations tomorrow.
The prime minister said Malaysia's continued engagement with Iran had also yielded practical benefits, particularly in safeguarding the country's fuel supply routes through the strategically important Strait of Hormuz.
"When we faced difficulties involving our ships and oil supply passing through the Strait of Hormuz, Malaysia was among the first allowed through.
"The early shipments successfully crossed the strait and arrived in Pengerang," he said.
Anwar said the uninterrupted movement of Malaysian oil shipments had helped maintain fuel supply stability despite uncertainties affecting global energy markets and shipping routes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Snatch theft sentences: Why courts are rejecting lighter jail terms for robbery
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Snatch theft and robbery warrant serious punishment because their impact extends far beyond monetary losses, crime analyst Datuk Shahul Hamid Abdul Rahim said.
Backing the Court of Appeal's stance that offenders should face stiff penalties, he said such crimes instil fear and insecurity among the public, particularly women, senior citizens and other vulnerable groups.
Victims, he said, often suffer not only the loss of valuables but also lasting psychological effects, with many becoming afraid to walk alone, use public transport or carry personal belongings in public.
"Such crimes can erode public confidence and diminish overall quality of life if left unchecked," he said when contacted.
Shahul Hamid was commenting on the Court of Appeal's decision to reinstate a seven-year jail sentence on a former factory worker who pleaded guilty to robbery.
A three-member panel led by Justice Noorin Badaruddin unanimously allowed the prosecution's appeal, overturning the High Court's decision to reduce the sentence to four years and six months.
The panel, which also comprised Justices Datuk Hayatul Akmal Abdul Aziz and Datuk Seri Mohd Radzi Harun, restored the original seven-year sentence imposed by the Sessions Court.
While tougher penalties could serve as a strong deterrent, particularly against repeat offenders, Shahul Hamid said punishment alone would not solve the problem.
"Many robbery and snatch theft cases are linked to factors such as drug addiction, unemployment, economic pressures and opportunistic criminal behaviour," he said.
He also pointed to enforcement challenges, noting that such offences occur nationwide but suspects are not always swiftly apprehended, with some cases remaining unsolved due to a lack of evidence or eyewitnesses.
Public cooperation, he added, is vital in helping police solve cases, as witness accounts and community information often determine whether investigations succeed.
He urged police to reassess high-risk areas and intensify patrols at crime hotspots to improve early detection and arrests, saying swift enforcement action would strengthen public confidence and enhance perceptions of safety.
Meanwhile, former Malaysian Bar president Salim Bashir said a guilty plea in serious robbery and snatch theft cases should not automatically result in lighter punishment, as sentencing depends on the facts and circumstances of each case.
He said courts must balance deterrence, public interest and fairness before deciding on an appropriate sentence.
"A guilty plea in a serious offence does not guarantee discounts on sentencing. Reduced penalties very much depend on the distinct facts and circumstances of each case," he said.
Salim said sentencing principles were anchored on the need to express "abhorrence for heinous offences", deter offenders and would-be criminals, as well as reform the accused.
"The guiding principles must be weighed and balanced with aggravating factors on account of public interest," he said.
He added that courts would consider the gravity of offences, particularly those with significant implications for public safety.
"The amount of censure should be proportionate to the seriousness of the crime, more so if the act reflects wanton endangerment of human life," he said.
Although robbery was categorised as a property crime, Salim said courts would still assess its severity through several factors, including the possibility of death or serious injuries to victims.
He said courts would also consider the vulnerability and emotional trauma suffered by victims, as well as the "rampancy" of robbery offences.
He added that mitigating factors raised by the accused, as well as alternative punishments apart from imprisonment, should also be considered before heavier prison sentences are imposed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Son of Libya's Haftar vows to make up for 'lost years' under Gaddafi
Article:
BELGACEM Haftar, son of eastern Libya's military chief Khalifa Haftar, touted in an interview with AFP what he called a push to make up for more than 40 "lost years" under slain ruler Muammar Gaddafi.
After Gaddafi was killed during the 2011 revolution, Libya descended into civil war, splitting the country in half as the Haftar clan took power in the east.
The field marshal exercises tight security control in the area he rules and, now Belgacem, one of his six sons, heads a reconstruction programme and appears to be seeking to restore the clan's legitimacy.
Following the floods that swept through Derna in 2023, killing nearly 4,000 people, Belgacem became the figurehead for reconstruction efforts in the area.
Bridges, apartment blocks, schools and hospitals now rise in eastern Libya, which had long been marginalised under Gaddafi.
AFP journalists escorted by the Libyan National Army (LNA) led by Haftar saw dozens of construction sites in Benghazi, Derna and Bayda.
"Gaddafi governed Libya for 42 years, and he did not accomplish what we have in two years," Belgacem, 46, told AFP, seated in an office inside a lavish palace in Benghazi.
The oil-rich country remains divided between a UN-recognised government based in its capital, Tripoli, and the eastern administration in Benghazi backed by the Haftars.
The reconstruction fund was established in 2024 to rebuild Derna with a budget of some US$2 billion at the time, before expanding into the Libya Development and Reconstruction Fund.
Eastern Libya and most of the country's south are firmly controlled by Haftar and his sons.
Saddam Haftar, widely seen as his heir apparent, serves as deputy commander of the LNA, while another son, Khaled, is chief of staff.
Libya holds Africa's largest oil reserves at around 48.4 billion barrels, and areas controlled by the family contain most of the oil fields and export terminals.
The reconstruction fund has launched a wave of projects in areas under the control of the eastern administration, aiming to "make up for the lost years" of Gaddafi's reign, Belgacem said.
He said that since Gaddafi's overthrow, sectors such as education and healthcare as well as basic needs have been "completely neglected."
The Haftars' development push comes as they seek to showcase an ability to govern even beyond their areas of control, while the United States reportedly tries to promote a rapprochement between the country's rival authorities.
[[nid:1448866]]
Responding to accusations that the Haftars are using the fund without oversight and of financial misconduct, Belgacem said:
"The number and scale of the projects underway answer the question of where the money is being spent."
"The size of the projects and transparency are reflected in what we have achieved on the ground," he added.
He said every contract signed by the fund is currently "audited by the appropriate authorities" while all payments are processed through the central bank in Tripoli.
Libya has long faced repression and poverty, both during and after Gaddafi's rule.
Belgacem said the fund has "mended trust between citizens and the state by implementing projects that had been abandoned for years."
On Friday, Belgacem attended a rehearsal for an education sector celebration at Benghazi's newly built stadium.
After posing for selfies with parents and children, a young girl approached him carrying requests from her family on a small piece of paper.
Beyond areas already under the Haftars' control, the reconstruction fund could expand into western Libya, "if the authorities request it", Belgacem said.
He said the region housing the capital, Tripoli, and a large proportion of the Libyan population was in "immense need" for infrastructure.
But tensions between Belgacem and Prime Minister Abdelhamid Dbeibah, who leads the Tripoli-based government, would need to ease off.
During the interview, Belgacem accused Dbeibah of having "carried out no project on the scale of the Libyan state in the last five years."
And Dbeibah has charged that the eastern administration has been spending off budget in the past three years.
Still, there have been signs of a potential shift.
Last month, the legislative bodies of the rival authorities signed a US-mediated agreement to unify public spending across the divided country for the first time in over a decade.
The deal included investments worth some 20 billion dinars (US$3 billion) in western Libya, Belgacem told AFP.
A similar amount was also allocated to the east and south, where the fund seeks to develop further "strategic projects", he added.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC cadet corps in schools key to early anti-graft education, says CJ
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Chief Justice Datuk Seri Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh has proposed the establishment of Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) cadet corps in schools as part of efforts to instil anti-corruption values from a young age.
He said early anti-corruption education should begin at the school level, similar to existing uniformed bodies such as police and army cadet corps.
He said the proposed MACC cadet corps could help nurture a strong sense of integrity and aversion to corruption among students, taking inspiration from Japan's education model, which prioritises integrity and courtesy before academic learning.
"If we want to educate society, it must begin at school level, and I encourage the establishment of MACC cadet corps so that anti-corruption values can be instilled through early education.
"At present, the public sees MACC as focusing mainly on enforcement, although the commission has already implemented many preventive efforts," he said after receiving a courtesy call from MACC Chief Commissioner Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman at the Palace of Justice here yesterday.
Meanwhile, Halim said the MACC has been carrying out preventive education programmes through its Community Education Division to spread anti-corruption messages among school and university students.
"When they enter primary and secondary schools, we will place special emphasis on terms such as 'duit kopi'.
"This is important so that they understand such acts are forms of corruption," he said.
He said the MACC had already established the Anti-Corruption Student Volunteer Corps (Kor SUAR) at university level, with Universiti Sultan Zainal Abidin (UniSZA) in Terengganu becoming the pioneer public university for the initiative.
He said the educational strategy would gradually be expanded to primary and secondary schools, focusing on public understanding of terms and practices often regarded as trivial by society.
He added that anti-corruption education is a long-term commitment that should continue through university level to ensure the message does not stop after schooling years.
On improving outreach, the MACC acknowledged the need to strengthen publicity efforts and enhance the delivery of anti-corruption messages to the public.
Halim said the commission plans to move away from conventional methods such as posters and lectures, and instead focus on dialogue sessions and interactive forums involving MACC officers and members of the public.
"I strongly encourage dialogue sessions so that we can obtain direct input from the rakyat on the ground.
"We want to know the public's expectations of the MACC so that the feedback can be used to improve the existing system," he said.
On the courtesy call to the Chief Justice, Halim said both the MACC and the judiciary remain committed to strengthening strategic cooperation to ensure smoother corruption trials, including fully supporting the establishment of a more efficient Special Corruption Court in Kuala Lumpur.
He said collaboration between enforcement agencies and the courts is crucial in expediting corruption case hearings, in line with the Yang di-Pertuan Agong's call to strengthen the nation's judicial system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Enhanced radio network, air assets deployed in search for missing hiker Jaslinda
Article:
TAPAH: The search and rescue (SAR) operation for missing hiker Jaslinda Saludin, 49, entered its next phase today with enhanced communications support from the Government Integrated Radio Network (GIRN) at the Kuala Woh control post.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department assistant director (operations) Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said the SAR operation is focusing on routes leading to Gunung Rayu and Pos Musoh, which are believed to be among the paths Jaslinda may have taken.
"Equipment and communication systems will be enhanced with the presence of Sapura, the party responsible for the GIRN system at the control post," he said in a statement.
Today's operation deployed an AW139 aircraft from the department's Northern Region Air Base, with 12 rescuers airlifted to reinforce eight personnel who had spent the night at the summit of Gunung Batu Putih.
Sabarodzi added that the primary control post remains at Kuala Woh, while the forward control post managing aircraft operations is stationed at the General Operations Force 3rd Battalion Camp in Bidor.
Jaslinda was reported to have started the Trans Spencer Chapman expedition at 2am on May 23 via the Pos Gedung-Gunung Bah Gading-Gunung Batu Putih-Kuala Woh route.
She and another climber, Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41, reportedly suffered health complications and stopped to rest.
Jaslinda is believed to have continued the trek toward the summit alone and was last seen by a Forestry Mountain Guide member at 7.30am on Sunday.
Mohd Hanafi was successfully evacuated by an air rescue team yesterday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: South Korea to step up monitoring of investments in overseas private debt
Article:
SEOUL: South Korea will step up monitoring investments by domestic pension funds and banks in overseas private debt, the country's financial watchdog said on Tuesday, as regulators worldwide become more concerned about private credit funds.
The outstanding balance of private credit investments by Korean pension funds and other government-controlled retirement funds rose 55.3 per cent to 25.4 trillion won (US$16.86 billion) as of the end of February, from 16.3 trillion won in 2023, South Korea's Financial Supervisory Service (FSS) said in a statement.
Local financial institutions, including brokerages, insurers and credit unions, had about 30.5 trillion won worth of exposure to private debt as of February, the FSS said.
The bulk of South Korea's exposure was to debt in the United States or Europe, the FSS said. While local banks had limited exposure to credit tied to the technology sector, state-run funds had 21.8 per cent exposure to the sector, the watchdog said.
The announcement comes as global regulators tighten scrutiny over potential risks from the US$3.5 trillion private credit industry.
Wealthy investors have queued up to withdraw money from a popular category of private credit funds amid concerns that AI could disrupt the industry. The Financial Times also reported last week that HSBC had paused a US$4 billion plan to invest in its own private credit ​funds.
Still, the investment risk was at a "manageable level", the FSS said, noting liquidity risks were not too high. South Korean financial firms' exposure to private credit products accounted for less than one per cent of their total asset value, while government-run funds also had a similar level of risk, it said.
These Korean funds are mostly closed-end, which means investors cannot request redemptions before maturity, according to the FSS.
The watchdog will monitor the situation closely for a while considering the market trend, the FSS said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vape murder: Three brothers charged over fatal playground stabbing
Article:
JASIN: Three brothers were charged in the magistrate's court here today with the murder of a 30-year-old man near a playground two weeks ago.
The accused – R. Murugan, 20; R. Arunasalam, 27; and R. Magendran, 28 – nodded in understanding after the charge was read out in Tamil before magistrate Mazana Sinin.
No plea was recorded as murder cases fall under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
According to the charge, the three men allegedly murdered Richard Edward near a playground along Jalan Anum 3, Taman Muhibbah, Merlimau, between 8pm and 8.30pm on May 13.
They were charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty, or imprisonment of between 30 and 40 years and no fewer than 12 strokes of the cane, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Syazwani Yusri appeared for the prosecution, while lawyer L. Sherveen represented all three accused.
The court fixed July 27 for the next case mention pending the submission of the chemistry and pathology reports.
Earlier, media reports said the victim succumbed to stab wounds sustained during a dispute over a transaction involving "mushroom-flavoured" vape liquid at a playground in Merlimau.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Quad' announces new Asia projects after questions on US
Article:
NEW DELHI: The United States, India, Australia and Japan announced new maritime and critical minerals cooperation on Tuesday, reviving a forum viewed suspiciously by China following questions over the US commitment and disagreement on Iran.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio took part in the meeting in New Delhi, 10 days after President Donald Trump paid a friendly state visit to China and spoke glowingly of the two powers working together as a "G2" – a concept that the US allies, which view Beijing's rise warily, fear could shut them out.
Meeting his counterparts, Rubio said the Quad comprised countries "who share strong values – strong, vibrant democracies – who also are committed to many of the same concepts with regards to economic development and have many aligned interests."
Rubio said the four powers would work together on two maritime initiatives – one that combines their surveillance capabilities, and another that will provide enhanced real-time information to commercial traffic at sea.
In a first, Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong said the Quad was cooperating on assisting port development in Fiji – a key island nation in the South Pacific, where China has made a concerted push for greater influence.
Rubio said the Quad would also step up four-way efforts to secure the supply of critical minerals, a rare area in which the Trump administration has turned to the more traditional diplomacy of building networks with allies, alarmed at the dominance of China in resources key to the high-end technology sector.
Trump, since taking office, has repeatedly been dismissive of major allies, saying they unfairly burden the United States financially.
While Rubio held two Quad meetings last year, including hours after taking office, Trump declined to commit to a four-way leaders' summit.
It marked a strong contrast to his predecessor Joe Biden, who put a top priority on alliances and vowed that Quad summits were "here to stay."
Rubio said the Trump administration wanted the Quad to focus more on deliverables than meetings, and said cooperation was progressing "pretty aggressively."
Freedom of navigation has long been Washington's codeword for opposing China's assertiveness at sea, a particular concern for Japan.
But the United States has recently pointed to the principle as it tries to rally allies to counter Iran, which has exerted control over the strategic Strait of Hormuz in response to a US-Israeli war launched on Feb 28, sending global oil prices spiralling.
No US ally other than Israel has robustly supported the decision to attack Iran, enraging Trump, who has questioned the reliability of US partners, whom he had not consulted beforehand.
India's Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said the focus of the talks would be "the Indo-Pacific, which is the specific limit of the Quad."
Besides Iran, India has differed with the other Quad nations on Russia's invasion of Ukraine by refusing to sever its long-standing relationship with Moscow.
Australia's Wong said there was much to focus on in Asia, due to its "deteriorating strategic environment and acute economic stress."
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese is one of the few world leaders who has voiced some understanding for the Iran war, citing concerns about its disputed nuclear programme, but he has not assisted the war effort and Trump said he was "not happy with Australia."
Japan and India both historically have maintained cordial relations with Iran, although they grudgingly complied with US sanctions aimed at stopping all other countries from buying Iranian oil.
Japanese Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi, holding a bilateral meeting with India on Monday, said it was important to address an "increasingly severe" global security situation.
"The world is faced with the most significant structural change in the post-World War II era, driven by a shift in the balance of power and the intensification of conflict and confrontation," Motegi said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar assures Malaysians of sufficient fuel supply, says RON95 to remain at RM1.99
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has assured Malaysians that the country's fuel supply remains stable, with RON95 petrol to stay at RM1.99 per litre despite ongoing geopolitical tensions.
Anwar said Malaysia was still receiving sufficient fuel supplies, including diesel, supported by strong diplomatic relations with countries in the region and the Middle East.
He said this had enabled Malaysian oil shipments to navigate the strategically important Strait of Hormuz, even as tensions escalated following attacks linked to the Zionist Israeli regime and its allies in Gaza and Lebanon.
"When we faced difficulties involving our ships and oil supply passing through the Strait of Hormuz, Malaysia was among the first allowed through.
"The early shipments successfully arrived in Pengerang, and Alhamdulillah, our fuel supply situation remains under control," he said in a speech.
Anwar earlier presented 260 cows for korban to communities throughout the Permatang Pauh parliamentary constituency in conjunction with Aidiladha celebrations tomorrow.
He said several countries were already facing fuel shortages and long queues at petrol stations, citing reports from the Philippines during his recent visit to Cebu for the Asean meeting.
He said Malaysia had so far been spared such disruptions due to adequate planning and strong international ties.
"Some petrol stations elsewhere have closed, and people are queueing for fuel, but Alhamdulillah, we have not reached that stage.
"We pray the situation remains stable," he added.
Anwar said the government expected fuel supplies to remain sufficient at least through July.
He acknowledged that global uncertainties and rising shipping insurance costs had contributed to increases in transportation and import costs, particularly for diesel.
"Oil prices and shipping costs have risen because supplies have to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, and insurance premiums have also increased.
"But as far as we can manage, RON95 remains at RM1.99," he stressed.
The prime minister also defended Malaysia's fuel subsidy system, noting that only Brunei offered cheaper RON95 prices in the region.
Anwar acknowledged that despite the increase in diesel prices, the government had introduced various assistance initiatives under the Budi Madani programme to help affected groups, including farmers, fishermen, tractor operators, public transport providers and school bus operators.
He admitted that the measures might not fully address all concerns, as the country continued to face economic and global challenges, but stressed that the government was doing what it could to ease the burden on the rakyat.
He also called on Malaysians to remain resilient, united and grateful, saying the country's situation was still better compared with several neighbouring nations.
While acknowledging the people's right to criticise and demand improvements, he urged Malaysians to also appreciate the stability and assistance they continued to receive.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One-way flights to Sabah, Sarawak capped at RM499 for Kaamatan, Gawai period
Article:
KAJANG: The government has set a maximum price cap of RM499 for one-way economy class flight tickets from Peninsular Malaysia to Sabah and Sarawak for three days preceding the Kaamatan and Hari Gawai festivals.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the government will subsidise the difference between the actual market price set by airlines and the capped fare.
"It is now standard for festive season flights to Sabah and Sarawak to be capped at RM499.
"This is not borne by the airlines, as the government has to pay a top-up to the airline companies to ensure they only charge a maximum of RM499 for those three days before the festivals.
"The subsidy provided by the ministry will increase because if, for instance, a ticket is priced at RM1,000, the maximum price a traveller pays is RM499. The government will then subsidise the remaining RM501 to the airline," he told reporters after visiting Carro Technology Sdn Bhd's Kajang vehicle inspection centre here today.
He said the capped rate only applies to one-way travel to East Malaysia before the celebrations, as return travel dates are usually spread out and do not experience the same peak demand.
Kaamatan is celebrated annually on May 30 and May 31 in Sabah, while Hari Gawai is celebrated on June 1 and June 2 in Sarawak.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hong Leong Bank Q3 profit tops RM1bil on loan growth, stable margins
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Bank Bhd posted a net profit of RM1.02 billion for the third quarter ended March 31, 2026, compared with RM946.69 million a year earlier.
Its quarterly revenue also rose 2.1 per cent to RM1.58 billion from RM1.54 billion, according to its Bursa Malaysia filing.
No dividend was declared for the quarter.
For the nine-month period (9M FY26), Hong Leong Bank's net profit rose to RM3.28 billion from RM3.18 billion a year earlier, while revenue also increased to RM4.93 billion compared with RM4.77 billion previously.
"Total income for 9M FY26 was 3.2 per cent year-on-year (YoY) higher at RM4.9 billion, attributable to loans/financing portfolio expansion and prudent funding cost management, which saw net interest margin stable at 1.83 per cent.
"Non-interest income grew 2.2 per cent YoY, buoyed by an increase in wealth management activities and global markets franchise sales," it said.
Hong Leong Bank said its gross loans, advances and financing continued to grow, rising 8.4 per cent YoY to RM218.2 billion.
This was driven by expansion in its core segments, including mortgages, auto loans, small and medium enterprises and commercial banking, as well as contributions from key overseas markets.
Hong Leong Bank group managing director and chief executive officer Kevin Lam said the bank's priority is to strengthen its core operations by transforming its branch network, deepening strategic partnerships, and enhancing artificial intelligence and digital capabilities.
He added that the bank remains committed to embedding environmental, social and governance principles across its strategies and operations to deliver long-term value for communities and stakeholders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-national footballer, motivational speaker settle fines to reclaim seized luxury cars
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A former national footballer has retrieved his luxury Toyota Vellfire after settling all outstanding fines and offences that led to its seizure by the Road Transport Department (RTD).
Kuala Lumpur RTD confirmed that the athlete settled his traffic violations and was permitted to reclaim the multi-purpose vehicle yesterday.
Meanwhile, a motivational speaker holding a "Datuk" title, whose high-end vehicle was also impounded during the same operation, is in the process of securing its release after settling his respective penalties.
The duo were stopped during a recent RTD crackdown code-named Ops Luxury.
The former national footballer, celebrated for helping his team clinch multiple Super League championships, was intercepted behind the wheel of his luxury MPV at 4am last Saturday in Bukit Bintang.
The motivational speaker was pulled over in the Klang Valley while driving a Mercedes-Benz Brabus AMG with an expired road tax and no insurance coverage.
RTD deputy director-general (planning and operations) Datuk Jazmanie Shafawi had said that the former footballer's vehicle was impounded after officers flagged three distinct offences: driving without insurance, an expired road tax and an expired driving licence.
"Our checks revealed that the vehicle's road tax had expired since Dec 7 last year.
"It had gone unrenewed for five months, which is why the vehicle was impounded," he told a press conference at the Kuala Lumpur RTD storage depot here yesterday.
Jazmanie added that the motivational speaker's car was seized because its road tax had expired a month prior and lacked insurance coverage, creating severe safety and financial liabilities for other road users.
"During the inspection, we also discovered the vehicle was displaying licence plates that belonged to another car.
"The driver had legally changed the vehicle registration number but continued to display the old plates, which constitutes a serious offence," Jazmanie said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: E&O FY26 profit jumps 31pct to RM221.2mil on record property sales
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Eastern & Oriental Bhd (E&O) reported a net profit of RM62.05 million for the fourth quarter ended March 31, 2026 (Q4 FY26), down 11.2 per cent from RM69.85 million a year ago.
Revenue for the quarter slipped marginally to RM235.9 million from RM236.7 million previously.
For the full financial year ended March 31, 2026 (FY26), E&O's net profit surged 31.2 per cent to RM221.2 million from RM168.6 million a year earlier, while revenue rose 17 per cent to RM867.6 million from RM741.1 million previously.
The property segment remained the key contributor to performance during the year, recording a 20 per cent increase in revenue to RM753.9 million, accounting for 87 per cent of the total developer's revenue.
The stronger performance was supported by healthy construction progress and sales contributions from developments across Andaman Island in Penang as well as projects in the Klang Valley.
E&O also achieved record property sales of RM1.4 billion in FY26, while unbilled sales stood at RM1.7 billion, providing strong earnings visibility moving forward.
Managing director Kok Tuck Cheong said the group closed FY26 with encouraging operational momentum, marked by record revenue, profitability, property sales and unbilled sales performance.
"As we continue to advance the Andaman Island masterplan and strengthen our development pipeline, our focus remains on disciplined execution, product differentiation and delivering long-term value to our stakeholders," he said.
The company aims to launch two additional residential projects in the coming financial year while continuing to explore commercial development opportunities within Andaman Island.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Amirudin replaces Nurul Izzah as PKR election director [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: PKR has appointed its vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari as the party's election director alongside Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail, replacing deputy president Nurul Izzah Anwar.
PKR secretary-general Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said the appointment was part of the party's strategic restructuring to ensure its election machinery is further strengthened, better coordinated and implemented more comprehensively at all levels.
"The restructuring also builds on groundwork laid previously, including strengthening the election structure, improving coordination between state and division levels, and expanding the use of data, systems and technology in field operations through the introduction of the TERAS election system," she said in a statement today.
She said the decision was made after the party's political bureau meeting yesterday.
Fuziah said Nurul Izzah would continue to play a strategic role in the next phase, with a specific focus on strengthening party machinery and organisational operations towards a more robust and structured framework.
"This focus forms part of the party's investment in its grassroots members to ensure PKR's strength continues to be built comprehensively, sustainably and across all levels."
In addition to Amirudin's appointment, Azman Abidin was appointed director of the war room, assisted by Ruji Ubi, to strengthen coordination of election strategies, field operations and monitoring of campaign machinery.
Fuziah said PKR's new memberships reached 5,936 between April and May.
"PKR is now moving into its next phase with a firm commitment to close ranks, strengthen grassroots capabilities and ensure the entire party machinery operates as a focused, organised and election-ready team ahead of the next polls.
"PKR recognises that the party's strength lies not only in its central leadership, but also in the thousands of members on the ground who work tirelessly, engage with communities and champion the people's concerns every day."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hong Leong Financial Q3 net profit up 4.8pct to RM748.7mil on stronger divisional performance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd's net profit grew 4.8 per cent to RM748.7 million from RM714 million, supported by stronger contributions across all operating divisions.
However, revenue slipped 0.6 per cent to RM1.69 billion from RM1.7 billion.
For the nine-month period, the bank's net profit rose 3.7 per cent to RM2.49 billion from RM2.4 billion, while revenue climbed 1.8 per cent to RM5.55 billion from RM5.45 billion.
In a statement, the company said its commercial banking division, Hong Leong Bank Bhd, achieved operating profit growth of six per cent.
Inclusive of profit contribution from its associate, Hong Leong Bank's pre-tax profit recorded growth of 0.3 per cent as a result of the natural dilution of the bank's stake in its associated company, Bank of Chengdu Co. Ltd, following the completion of its convertible bonds' conversion and a stronger ringgit.
Meanwhile, insurance division HLA Holdings Sdn Bhd's pre-tax profit improved by 5.2 per cent, driven by improved investment income from equities and higher contributions from associate MSIG Insurance (Malaysia) Bhd.
This offset weaker net insurance service results caused by higher claims.
Its investment banking and fund management division, Hong Leong Capital Bhd, also recorded a 4.2 per cent increase in pre-tax profit due to stronger contributions from treasury and markets and higher mark-to-market gains in equity investments.
Annualised return on average equity remains commendable at 10.1 per cent, while book value per share increased to RM29.6 from RM27.8 a year ago.
Hong Leong Financial Group president and chief executive officer Tan Kong Khoon said the company remains well positioned to pursue future opportunities while remaining vigilant of external headwinds stemming from geopolitical uncertainties.
He said the company continues to evolve its wealth management suite through innovation.
"This quarter, our commercial bank has rebranded its Islamic and Priority banking divisions to HLB Islamic and HLB Priority, marking a shift towards a more integrated, wealth-focused service model.
"Broadening this wealth ecosystem, our life insurance arm debuted 'Smart Legacy', a legacy planning product for high-net-worth individuals featuring a RM2 million minimum sum assured.
"Additionally, our investment bank has rolled out new bond investment financing solutions tailored for sophisticated investors," Tan added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: The palm oil peak is closing in sooner than we think
Article:
For decades, palm oil has been the quiet workhorse of the global economy. It's the invisible ingredient in everything from your morning shampoo and the chocolate spread on your toast to the biodiesel in a truck traversing Europe.
Its ascension to become the world's largest traded edible oil was a story of relentless expansion, driven by its unrivalled productivity. No other crop comes close to yielding as much oil per hectare. This efficiency made it price competitive and ubiquitous.
After years of seemingly limitless growth, the engine is sputtering. Malaysia, the number two producer, has seen its output plateau, a victim of geographical reality and stagnant agricultural yields. And now Indonesia, the colossus that accounts for roughly 60 per cent of global supply, is also showing signs of "production fatigue". While the supply side is hitting a wall, global demand for oils and fats continues its relentless 3.0 per cent annual climb. This is not just a problem for the vegetable oil traders; it's a potential crisis for the planet. How will the world cope when its most productive oil crop can no longer expand to meet our insatiable appetite?
The first possibility is a permanent upward shift in the price of everything. Palm oil isn't just another commodity; it's a price setter for the entire oils and fats complex. When palm is expensive, soybean oil, sunflower oil, and rapeseed oil become expensive, too. A supply crunch in palm oil will ripple through the global food system, inflating costs for food manufacturers and, ultimately, for consumers. For the billions of people in developing nations who rely on cooking oil as a dietary staple, this isn't an inconvenience; it's a threat to their daily nutrition.
The market's natural response to higher prices is to encourage more supply. But where will it come from? The immediate pressure will fall on other oilseed crops. We can expect to see a frantic scramble to plant more soybeans in the Brazilian Cerrado and more rapeseed in the Canadian prairies. However, this is a deeply flawed solution. To replace the lost output of a single palm oil plantation, you need vastly more land planted with these alternative crops.
This isn't intensification; it's extensification at its most destructive. It would mean ploughing up carbon-rich grasslands, accelerating deforestation in the Amazon and other sensitive biomes, and devouring vast tracts of land that could otherwise be used for food security or left for nature. We risk solving a supply problem by creating an ecological catastrophe of a different, perhaps even greater, magnitude.
Another avenue is to squeeze more from the land we already have. The "stagnant yield levels" in Malaysia and Indonesia are a damning indictment. For too long, the industry has relied on area expansion as its primary growth strategy. The low-hanging fruit is gone. Now, the focus must shift to agricultural science. This means a massive investment in developing higher-yielding, disease-resistant palm varieties, optimising fertiliser use, and improving planting techniques. It also means confronting the age-old problem of plantation management, particularly helping the myriad of smallholder farmers who manage a significant portion of the world's palm oil acreage to close the yield gap between their output and what is biologically possible.
We must also address the demand side of the equation. The 3.0 per cent annual growth is a measure of our consumption habits. We need a global conversation about our reliance on vegetable oils. This isn't about asking consumers in developing nations to eat less but about tackling the massive, often hidden, use of these oils in the developed world—in processed foods, in cosmetics, and, most controversially, in biofuels.
The diversion of palm oil into fuel tanks is the ultimate perversion of a food system. As supply tightens, the logic of burning food in our cars becomes increasingly untenable. Policymakers must urgently re-evaluate their biofuel mandates. When the world's most efficient oil crop can no longer keep up, using it to power vehicles while people face higher food prices is a policy choice that is difficult to defend.
The end of the era of expanding palm oil production is the present reality. How we respond will define our food systems for generations. We can stumble into a future of volatile prices and food insecurity. Or we can take a more intelligent path: one that combines a herculean effort to boost yields sustainably on existing farmland with a serious and honest attempt to moderate demand, starting with the foolishness of putting food in our fuel tanks.
The world's appetite for oils and fats is not going away, but the way we satisfy it must fundamentally change. The era of easy expansion is over. The era of hard choices has begun.
---
The author is affiliated with the Tan Sri Omar Centre for STI Policy Studies at UCSI University and is an adjunct professor at the Ungku Aziz Centre for Development Studies, Universiti Malaya.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man who stabbed woman in the neck ordered to undergo psychiatric evaluation [WATCH]
Article:
ALOR STAR: A 32-year-old real estate agent pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court today to attempting to murder his female friend by stabbing her in the neck with a Rambo knife on May 17.
Muhammad Ilhami Badaruddin claimed trial after the charge was read to him before judge N. Priscilla Hemamalini.
According to the charge sheet, Ilhami is accused of attempting to murder Nurul Husna Kasmani, 35, by stabbing her in the neck in the parking lot of a restaurant in Titi Gajah, Kepala Batas, at 9.19pm on May 17.
He was charged under Section 307(1) of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum sentence of 20 years and a fine upon conviction.
State prosecution director Datuk Khairul Anuar Abdul Halim, along with deputy public prosecutors Muhammad Fahmi Kamaluddin and Vatchira Wong Rui Fern, appeared for the prosecution while the accused was represented by Mohd Hakim Hamran.
Hakim informed the court that his client suffers from mental health issues and had previously received psychiatric treatment at Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital here.
Following the submission, the court ordered the accused to be sent to Hospital Bahagia in Ulu Kinta, Perak, for a psychiatric evaluation.
The court denied bail and fixed July 2 for mention and the submission of documents.
It was previously reported that during the 9.19pm incident, the 35-year-old victim sustained severe neck injuries after being
stabbed inside a car
in front of the restaurant.
The suspect fled the scene but was arrested by police four hours later. The weapon was recovered the following day in a nearby padi field.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DoE defends EIA process after transparency concerns
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Department of Environment (DoE) has defended its handling of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) reports following criticism by an environmental watchdog over transparency and public access to the documents.
The department said that all EIA-related procedures are conducted in accordance with processes certified under the MS ISO 9001:2015 standard to ensure reviews and decisions are carried out professionally, systematically and with accountability.
The DoE said that since January this year, with support from the Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Ministry (NRES), it had continued providing public access to executive summaries of First Schedule and Second Schedule EIA reports.
The summaries can be accessed through the Enviro Digital Library@DoE website at https://edl.doe.gov.my/.
Members of the public seeking access to full EIA reports may submit an official application form available on the department's website to the DoE headquarters or state offices.
The department said access to the reports would then be arranged by appointment at the DoE Library.
"This approach ensures a balance between public transparency and DoE's responsibility to manage official government documents in a controlled and responsible manner.
"It also takes into account security aspects, document ownership rights and compliance with current policies and regulations," the department said.
The DoE also clarified that its role is to assess, review and consider EIA reports based on technical requirements, legal compliance and environmental impact before projects are approved.
It said the department would continue strengthening document management and EIA-related services in a professional, transparent and responsible manner to safeguard environmental sustainability and public interest, in line with the Environmental Quality Act 1974 (Act 127) and existing regulations.
Recently, an environmental watchdog reportedly criticised the DoE's handling of EIA reports, raising concerns over transparency and public access after allegedly facing difficulties accessing EIA documents through the department's online database.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia halts short selling in Critical Holdings
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Short selling of Critical Holdings Bhd's shares has been suspended for the rest of the day after its share price plunged more than 17 per cent on Tuesday.
The counter's share price dropped as much as 17.8 per cent to 83 sen in the morning trading session after recording lower earnings in the third quarter ended March 31, 2026.
The stock opened 6.9 per cent lower at 94 sen versus its closing price of RM1.01 on Monday.
It last traded 9.4 per cent or 9.5 sen lower at 91.5 sen, with more than five million shares traded.
According to Bursa Malaysia, short selling of the stock will resume on Thursday.
The company's net profit plunged 88.1 per cent to RM1.2 million in the quarter under review from RM10.06 million a year prior on the back of lower revenue.
Its revenue dropped 47.9 per cent to RM50.8 million from RM97.6 million due to reduced contribution from the mechanical, electrical and process utilities engineering solutions segment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar says korban is about sacrifice, resilience and helping the needy
Article:
SEBERANG PERAI, May 26 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today said the spirit of sacrifice should extend beyond religious obligations and serve as a reminder of perseverance, social responsibility and helping those in need.
He said Malaysia has remained resilient despite economic uncertainty and global tensions.
He said the practice of sacrifice in Islam carried values of faith, patience and a commitment to improving society.
“The act of
korban
(sacrifice)
was not solely a personal form of worship but one that should also benefit the wider community, particularly the poor and needy,” he said in a speech at the Madani Korban Cattle Distribution Ceremony in Seberang Jaya here.
“Without sacrifice and the spirit of struggle, there is no meaning in life,” he added.
Anwar said worship should strengthen not only a person’s relationship with God but also with society.
He said the benefits of
korban
should be shared with neighbours, families and vulnerable communities rather than being confined within one’s household.
He also welcomed a new approach to korban programmes involving shared contributions from participants who could afford to do so, instead of relying entirely on government support.
He said such participation creates a greater sense of ownership and appreciation for the religious obligation.
As for current economic challenges, Anwar said Malaysia remained in a relatively stable position despite global uncertainties, including geopolitical tensions affecting energy supply chains.
He said Malaysia’s diplomatic ties had helped ensure sufficient petrol supplies despite concerns over disruptions involving shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz.
“Today, at 6pm, I am expecting a call from the President of Iran,” he said.
He emphasised that this relationship with Iran helps the country as a whole despite criticisms by certain parties labelling him as the “toilet prime minister” who only knows to build ties with other countries.
“When we face difficulties and our ships and oil need to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, we are among the first to be allowed through,” he pointed out.
He also urged Malaysians to remain grateful while continuing to provide criticism and feedback responsibly.
“We can criticise and give views, but we should also be thankful for the blessings we receive,” he said.

Title: Petrol supply stable until August, says Anwar
Article:
SEBERANG PERAI, May 26 — Malaysia’s petrol supply is expected to remain stable until at least August despite global disruptions affecting energy routes and rising shipping costs, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said today.
He said Malaysia’s fuel stability was supported by its international relations and continued access to key shipping routes, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz.
“When we face difficulties and our ships and oil need to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, we are among the first to be allowed through,” he said in his speech at the Madani Korban Cattle Distribution Ceremony in Seberang Jaya here.
Anwar said this had helped ensure continued fuel availability in Malaysia, including deliveries to Pengerang.
“So the early ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz arrived in Pengerang, and now petrol in Malaysia is available,” he said.
He added that in contrast, some countries were experiencing fuel shortages.
“In some countries, I was in Cebu in the Philippines for the Asean meeting, there were people queuing at petrol stations,”
“Some stations had already closed while others had long queues,” he said.
He said Malaysia has not reached that point and hoped that the country will remain safe in months to come.
Anwar said the situation showed the importance of maintaining strong international relationships to safeguard national energy security.
He said the government expected fuel supply stability to continue until July, possibly August.
“Diesel is sufficient,” he said, adding that earlier issues involved diesel shortages even when oil supplies were available.
However, he acknowledged that global shipping costs had risen due to geopolitical tensions affecting the Strait of Hormuz, which had also impacted insurance and freight costs.
“Have prices of goods increased a little? Yes, because we buy from outside sources and shipments pass through the Strait of Hormuz, so insurance costs rise and shipping costs also increase,” he said.
Despite this, Anwar said the government had maintained the price of RON95 at RM1.99 per litre under its Budi95 programme.
“Tell me which country is cheaper than Malaysia? Only Brunei, with its population of around half a million, that is the only country,” he said.
He said the government continued to provide targeted assistance through initiatives such as Budi Madani, supporting farmers, fishermen, school transport operators and small industries.
As for diesel, he said subsidies and assistance programmes remained in place despite ongoing fiscal pressure.
“We provide various Budi Madani projects for farmers, fishermen, tractors, buses, school buses and transport assistance,” he said.
Anwar said although the measures were not sufficient to resolve all challenges, they reflected the government’s effort to balance fiscal constraints with public support.
“Is it enough? It is not enough, because we still face problems,” he said.
He urged Malaysians to remain resilient and grounded in shared responsibility.
“What is the meaning of life if we do not climb and go through both ease and hardship together?” he said.
He added that Malaysians should remain open to criticism while still appreciating national stability.
“This does not mean we cannot be thankful or that we cannot criticise. We can criticise and we can give views,” he said.

Title: Missing 19-year-old girl found dead near hiking trail after four-day search
Article:
HULU SELANGOR, May 26 — A teenage girl who was believed to have lost her way while hiking at Bukit Changkat Asa here four days ago was found dead by rescuers today.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the body of Nur Izzati Humaira Azizul, 19, was found off the hiking trail by the search and rescue (SAR) team at about 11.59 am.
“The victim’s body was found about 500 metres from the last known location where she was seen and one kilometre from the incident control post.
“It took the SAR team about 20 minutes to bring the body to the control post using a stretcher,” he told Bernama when met at the control post.
The body was handed over to the police before being taken to the Kuala Kubu Bharu Hospital, he added.
Earlier, the SAR operation, which entered its fourth day today, resumed at 8 am involving various security agencies after being suspended at 6.30 pm yesterday.
Nur Izzati Humaira was believed to have lost her way at about 10.50 am last Saturday while descending the hill with her family after deciding to walk ahead alone as she felt hot and tired.
The case drew widespread attention on social media and in the news over the past few days, with many netizens expressing concern and offering prayers and well-wishes for the teenager’s safe return throughout the SAR operation. — Bernama

Title: Kedah realtor charged with stabbing single mum in neck sent for mental health assessment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 26 — A real estate agent accused of the attempted murder of a single mother in Alor Setar has been ordered to undergo a psychiatric evaluation at Hospital Bahagia Ulu Kinta in Tanjung Rambutan, Perak.
Ilhamni Badaruddin, 32, pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court in Alor Setar today to a charge of attempted murder under Section 307(1) of the Penal Code,
Sinar Harian
reported.
Judge N. Priscilla Hemamalini allowed Ilhamni to be referred to Hospital Bahagia following an application by his lawyer, Hakim Hamran.
Hakim had applied for a mental health assessment for his client, citing an alleged history of psychiatric and other health issues.
The prosecution did not object to the application.
Ilhamni is alleged to have stabbed a 35-year-old woman in the neck with a knife at a parking area outside a restaurant in Titi Gajah, Alor Setar, at 9:19pm on May 17. The woman sustained serious injuries but survived the attack.
Under Section 307(1) of the Penal Code, the accused faces up to 20 years’ imprisonment if convicted.
The court set July 2 for the next mention of the case. No bail was allowed.

Title: Zahid takes Malaysian Bar’s DNAA challenge to Federal Court
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR May 26 — Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi has applied for the Federal Court to set aside a Court of Appeal ruling that granted the Malaysian Bar leave to challenge the Attorney General’s (AG) decision to seek a discharge not amounting to an acquittal (DNAA) for the Umno president in his Yayasan Akalbudi corruption case.
Zahid, as the second respondent to the Bar’s application, filed five legal questions to meet the required threshold under Section 96 of the Courts of Judicature Act 1964,
Free Malaysia Today
reported.
Leave to appeal to the Federal Court is granted only if the case raises novel constitutional or legal questions of public importance being heard for the first time.
Lawyer Shahrul Fazli Kamarulzaman confirmed that the cause papers were filed at the Federal Court registry yesterday. The first respondent, the AG, is expected to file a separate leave application next week.
Zahid and the AG are seeking to reinstate the June 27, 2024, High Court ruling that initially dismissed the Bar’s leave application.
On May 7 this year, the Court of Appeal ruled that the Bar had crossed the threshold required for the merits of its case to be heard in the High Court.
Justice Faizah Jamaludin, who sat with Justices Lim Hock Leng and Nadzrin Wok Nordin, unanimously held that the Bar had an arguable case that was not frivolous.
